返回本批次

普通药检每年导致数万人被错误逮捕

Common drug tests lead to tens of thousands wrongful arrests a year

www.cnn.com 作者 rawgabbit 分数 116 评论 93 2026/04/05 19:54
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 从一名男子汽车上刮下来的鸟粪在药物测试中显示为可卡因。一名幼儿的骨灰被登记为甲基苯丙胺或摇头丸。
  2. 一位曾祖母的药物检测出可卡因呈阳性，这引发了长达 15 个月的法律噩梦，迫使她为自己的房屋再融资，并催生了一项可能在全国范围内开创先例的新州法律。
  3. 科罗拉多州刚刚颁布了全国第一部法律，禁止仅根据比色药物测试的结果进行逮捕，这是全国执法部门广泛使用的现场测试。
  4. 这些测试很受欢迎，因为它们便宜、便携，并且可以在短短几分钟内筛查药物。将所有可疑药物样本发送到州立实验室是不可行的，这会昂贵得多，并且可能需要数天或数周才能返回结果。
  5. 但宾夕法尼亚大学的研究人员发现，这些廉价的检测也会导致误报率惊人。

Original Summary

  1. Bird poop scraped off a man’s car appeared on a drug test as cocaine. A toddler’s ashes registered as methamphetamine or ecstasy.
  2. And a great-grandmother’s medicine tested positive for cocaine – spawning a 15-month legal nightmare, forcing her to refinance her home, and spurring a new state law that could set a precedent across the country.
  3. Colorado just enacted the nation’s first law banning arrests based solely on the results of colorimetric drug tests – a field test widely used by law enforcement across the country.
  4. The tests are popular because they’re cheap, portable and can screen for drugs in mere minutes. It’s just not feasible to send all suspected drug samples to state laboratories, which would be far more expensive and could take days or weeks to return results.
  5. But these inexpensive tests also lead to false positives at alarming rates, researchers from the University of Pennsylvania found.

评论（默认中文）

djoldman 2026/04/05 22:14
> 对于那些因比色测试而被捕的人，“超过 90% 的人正在接受认罪协议，因为他们无法承担留在监狱并等待六个月进行实验室测试的费用，”沃尔什说。

这涉及到一个我很想知道答案的问题：如果被指控犯罪的人不能放弃快速审判的权利，会发生什么 和 认罪交易被拒绝？

对于被告来说：那些资源匮乏的人将在审判中花更少的时间进行辩护。拒绝认罪协议将消除强迫较低严重程度的定罪认罪的可能性。

对于检察官来说：提起诉讼的时间更少。

对法院和监狱的好处：案件更加干净和开放，监狱清理速度更快。

据推测，这将导致更加合理的收费——更少的收费以及更高优先级且更容易证明的收费。

短期内，检察官将别无选择，只能撤销大量指控，因为他们将不堪重负。

编辑：这是一个有趣的数据点，看起来纽约市通过了一项法律，要求检察官在快速审判日期之前准备好所有证据。看起来它导致了很多低级内容的解雇：

https://datacollaborativeforjustice.org/wp-content/uploads/2...

> For those who get arrested due to colorimetric testing, “over 90% of people are taking a plea deal because they can’t afford to remain in jail and wait six months for laboratory tests,” Walsh said.

This touches on a question to which I'd love to know the answer: what would happen if those charged with crimes could not waive their right to a speedy trial and plea deals were disallowed?

For the accused: those with low resources would go to trial with less time to mount a defense. Disallowing plea deals would remove the possibility of coercing lower-severity conviction pleas.

For the prosecutor: less time to mount a prosecution.

Benefits to courts and jails: much cleaner and more open dockets, jails cleared out much quicker.

Presumably this would lead to more rational charges - fewer charges and charges that were higher priority and easier to prove.

In the short term, prosecutors would have no choice but to drop a huge number of charges as they would be overwhelmed.

EDIT: here's an interesting data point where it looks like NYC passed a law that required prosecutors to have all evidence ready prior to the speedy trial date. It seems like it drove a lot of dismissals of low level stuff:

https://datacollaborativeforjustice.org/wp-content/uploads/2...

在 HN 中查看
unyttigfjelltol 2026/04/06 00:56
这显然是效率、资源和激励方面的问题。早些时候的法庭日期是在泰坦尼克号沉没之后重新调整甲板上的椅子。

由于问题是关于 实验室 和 技术，并且 国家实验室 宣称自己太忙而无法伸张正义，解决方案是为这些决策者提供问责制。需要强调的是：记者阅读了警察用来指控犯罪的现场药物测试上的标签，并确定它们不适合这种用途；然而国家实验室 应该 一直向警方广播这些信息，不仅失败了，而且 拒绝 几个月来测试相同的文章，直到案件真正进入审判阶段。我知道这不会成为好的新闻，但这个故事应该是“实验室，实验室，实验室，国家实验室到底出了什么问题”。你无法用新的法律来解决这个问题，你需要更好的人来负责这些实验室。

It's obviously a problem with efficiency, resources and incentives. Earlier court dates is reshuffling deck chairs on the titanic, basically after the ship sank.

Since the problem is about labs and technology, and that the state labs declared themselves too busy to do justice, the solution is to provide accountability for those decision-makers. Just to reinforce: the reporter read the label on these field drug tests police were using to charge crimes, and ascertained that they were unfit for this use; yet the state lab which should have been broadcasting this information to the police, not only failed, but refused to test the same articles for months and months, until the case was literally at trial. I know it wouldn't make good press, but this story should be "labs, labs, labs, what in the world is wrong with state labs". You can't fix it with new laws, you need better people in charge of these labs.

在 HN 中查看
mikkupikku 2026/04/05 22:33
愤世嫉俗的结果：系统无法处理案件量，并且无法扩展，它只是拒绝裁决任何不涉及贵族的纠纷。 国家实际上放弃了平民之间的正义作为一项义务，而将其留给私刑自卫。
Cynical outcome: the system can't handle the case load and instead of scaling, it just refuses to adjudicate any dispute that doesn't involve a noble. Justice between commoners is effectively abandoned as a duty by the state, and left to vigilantism.

cogman10 2026/04/05 23:51
> 它只是拒绝裁决任何不涉及贵族的纠纷

哦，我有消息告诉你，那已经发生了。

任何遭遇过汽车被盗、自行车被盗或房屋被盗的人都可以告诉你，警察不会采取任何行动。

如果你看看凶杀案等严重犯罪，你会发现破案率约为 66%。 这是他们自我报告的清除率。 这不是成功的起诉。 这只是“我们已经对此进行了足够的调查，并裁定我们认为是这个人干的”。 如果你看看强奸等犯罪行为，情况会更糟。

警察实际上管辖的犯罪是财产犯罪。 专门针对贵族。 警察非常擅长应对商店被抢劫或针对富人和人脉广泛的人的犯罪行为。 从目标那里偷了 1000 美元，你就会把书扔给你。 在同一个目标面前偷一辆价值 1000 美元的自行车，警察会耸耸肩说他们对此无能为力。

> it just refuses to adjudicate any dispute that doesn't involve a noble

Oh I got news for you, that already happened.

Anyone that's had their car broken into, bike stolen, or house burgled can tell you that cops won't do anything.

And if you look at serious crimes like homicide, you'll find a clearance rate of about 66%. And that's their self reported clearance rate. It's not successful prosecution. That's just the "we've looked into this enough and have decreed we think this person did it". It's a lot worse if you look at crimes like rape.

The crimes that police actually police are property crimes. Specifically for the nobles. Cops are pretty good at responding to stores being robbed or a crime against a wealthy and well connected person. Steal $1000 from a target and you'll get the book thrown at you. Steal a $1000 bike in front of the same target and cops will shrug and say there's nothing they can do about it.

SoftTalker 2026/04/06 00:23
你的例子是有缺陷的，因为塔吉特有摄像头和保安人员监视商店扒手，当你走出门时他们会拘留你，他们会向检察官提供视频和目击者证词。这是一个扣篮案。

警察对那些设法在未被发现的情况下走出商店的扒手没有兴趣，因为没有针对他们的案件的依据。

Your example is flawed because Target has cameras and a security staff watching for shoplifters, and they will detain you as you walk out the door, and they will provide video and eyewitness testimony to the prosecutor. It's a slam dunk case.

The shoplifters who do manage to walk out undetected are of no interest to the police, as there's no basis for a case against them.

cogman10 2026/04/06 00:39
塔吉特百货外面设有安全摄像头，工作人员经常在推车周围走来走去。 这就是为什么我选择这个例子。 证据几乎相同。

在许多地方，杂货店的汽车经常被闯入。 然而，警察对这些案件采取任何行动是不寻常的。 这并不是因为缺乏证据，大多数杂货店在整个停车场都设有摄像头。

天哪，由于城市采用了大量的群体摄像机进行监控，今天这已经不再是一个借口了。 然而警察仍然对这种盗窃行为不采取任何行动。

Target has security cameras on the outside and staff constantly walking around wrangling carts. That's why I picked this exact example. The evidence is pretty much the same.

At many locations, cars at grocery stores get broken into pretty frequently. Yet it's unusual for cops to ever do anything about those cases. That's not due to a lack of evidence, most grocery stores have cameras throughout the lot.

Hell, it's even less of an excuse today due to the amount of surveillance via flock cameras cities have adopted. Yet cops still don't do a thing about this sort of theft.

在 HN 中查看
stackskipton 2026/04/06 01:31
如果盗窃未达到重罪级别，警察不会采取任何行动。大多数商店扒手只有达到重罪级别才会被捕。

他们因入店行窃重罪被捕的原因是大型商店礼品包装了这些定罪。他们观看了这些内容，追踪了他们偷窃的具体物品的 UPC 和价格，拥有所有视频/电子表格，并将其全部以 DVD 形式交给警察/检察官。这些案件对于检察官来说非常容易，因为定罪率约为 100%，而且所需的任何作证均由大型商店支付。

更重要的是，作为社会，我们不知道如何处理有反社会行为的人。我说的不是大事，而是仍然影响生活质量的低级问题。

Cops don't do anything about theft if it does not rise to level of a felony period. Most shoplifters don't get arrested until they hit felony level either.

Reason they get arrested for felony shoplifting is big box stores gift wrap those convictions. They have watched them, tracked exact items they have stolen down to UPC with price, have all the video/spreadsheet and it's all handed to cop/prosecutor on DVD. Those cases are so easy for prosecutor because conviction rate is ~100% and any testifying required is all paid for by big box store.

On bigger note, as society, we don't know how to handle people who have antisocial behavior. I'm not talking big stuff but low-level stuff that still impacts quality of life.

stackskipton 2026/04/06 02:19
因为警察很少追人。

绝大多数时候，商店扒手被捕是因为他们仍在“犯罪”现场。又名，他们进入大型商店，面部识别系统起火，下班警察接到通知或呼叫警察。他们出现，将这个人拘留并拿走他们的礼品包装。

在通知警察之前，偷自行车的人早已消失。

Because Cops rarely chase people.

Vast majority of time when shoplifters get arrested, it's because they are still at scene of the "crime". AKA, they enter big box store, facial recognition fires and off duty cop there is notified or cops are called. They show up, take the person into custody and get their gift wrap.

Person stealing the bike is long gone before cops are notified.

SoftTalker 2026/04/06 01:28
孩子们捡起手推车不会拘留任何人。正是拘留使其发挥作用。警察来接他们，他们有目击者和视频。工作轻松。

实际实施拘留的保安人员通常是下班后加班的 LEO。就连克罗格这里的出口处也有一名武装警察。这样他们就可以合法拘留你，甚至逮捕你。

确实，他们不关心外面的自行车。这不是他们的财产。

Kids picking up carts aren't going to detain anyone. It's the detaining that makes it work. The cops just come pick them up, they have eyewitnesses and video. Easy work.

The security guards that do the actual detaining are often off-duty LEO picking up extra hours. Even Kroger here has an armed officer at the exit door. So they can legally detain you and even arrest you.

True that they don't care about the bike outside. It's not their property.

hackable_sand 2026/04/06 04:11

在 HN 中查看
ahhhhnoooo 2026/04/06 00:44
他们不能拘留你。警察可以拘留您，Target 员工可以坚持要求您留下来，但他们无法轻易且实际地阻止您离开。

他们可以逮捕公民，这将允许他们拘留你。但如果他们错了，并且你没有犯下重罪，那么该警卫将因非法监禁而承担民事和刑事责任——这是一项相当严重的指控。大多数商店保安都不愿意冒着生命危险来保护公司对 20 美元洗发水或其他东西的利益。

They cannot detain you. Cops can detain you, Target staff can insist you have to stay, but they cannot easily and practically bar you from leaving.

They can initiate a citizen's arrest, which would permit them to detain you But if they are wrong, and you've committed no felony, that guard is civilly and criminally liable for false imprisonment -- a pretty serious charge. Most store security are unwilling to risk their own personal necks to protect the company's interest in $20 of shampoo or whatever.

在 HN 中查看
djoldman 2026/04/06 02:16
在伊利诺伊州，“店主”绝对可以拘留某人：

> (a) 拘留。任何有合理理由相信某人犯有零售盗窃罪的商家，可以出于以下全部或任何目的，以合理的方式并在合理的时间内拘留该人，在零售商业场所内外：

> (1) 要求身份证明；

> (2) 验证该身份；

> (3) 合理询问该人是否持有未购买的商品，并对此类商品的所有权进行合理调查；

> (4) 通知治安官员该人被拘留，并将该人移交治安官员看管；

> (5) 如果是未成年人，应立即采取合理措施将这一拘留通知其父母、监护人或其他对该未成年人的福利感兴趣的个人，以及由商家酌情决定的治安官员，并将该未成年人的监护权移交给该人。

https://ilga.gov/Documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/072000...

In IL, a "shopkeeper" can definitely detain someone:

> (a) Detention. Any merchant who has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed retail theft may detain the person, on or off the premises of a retail mercantile establishment, in a reasonable manner and for a reasonable length of time for all or any of the following purposes:

> (1) To request identification;

> (2) To verify such identification;

> (3) To make reasonable inquiry as to whether such person has in his possession unpurchased merchandise and to make reasonable investigation of the ownership of such merchandise;

> (4) To inform a peace officer of the detention of the person and surrender that person to the custody of a peace officer;

> (5) In the case of a minor, to immediately make a reasonable attempt to inform the parents, guardian or other private person interested in the welfare of that minor and, at the merchant's discretion, a peace officer, of this detention and to surrender custody of such minor to such person.

https://ilga.gov/Documents/legislation/ilcs/documents/072000...

FireBeyond 2026/04/06 05:33
哈，我的手机被偷了，并在 eBay 上出售。我发现它是因为在 eBay 上买的人得到了我的联系信息并要求我解锁手机，我拒绝了，他要求卖家退款并给了我卖家的信息，卖家住的地方离我大约十分钟路程。

我找到了我的手机。在我的手机以及我费心尝试的每部手机上，IMEI 校验和失败，因为最后两位数字已被调换。效果：卖家看起来“合法”（哈哈），而我通过搜索我的实际 IMEI 找不到列表。

他的列表页面上有什么？ 100 多部手机，大多数“激活已锁定”，全部“没有充电器、电缆、配件。

笔记本电脑也一样。 “没有充电器、没有数据线、没有配件”，很多“被锁”。

所有的价格都荒谬（例如“像新的”m3 pro MBP 500 美元）。

将此信息提供给警方。

他们一点也不在乎。

“嗯，他可能不是自己偷的……”（也就是说，即使他们也很确定这些东西都是偷的）。

“故意出售赃物不是犯罪吗？”

“……”

“……”

我不在乎。他们只有我一个人有一个灌篮案。我的手机被偷了，警方已向他们提交了报告，手机出现在加利福尼亚州，在 eBay 上购买手机的人向他们提供了卖家信息，这个人住在我附近。

没有。

Hah, my phone was stolen and being sold on eBay. I found it because the person who bought it on eBay got my contact info and asked me to unlock the phone, I refused, he demanded a refund from the seller and gave me the seller's info, who lived about ten minutes away from me.

I found my phone. On my phone, and each and every phone I bothered to try, the IMEI failed checksumming because the last two digits had been transposed. Effect: seller looked "legit" (hah), and I couldn't find the listing by searching my actual IMEI.

What was on his page of listings? 100+ phones, most "activation locked", all "no chargers, cables, accessories.

Same with laptops. "No chargers, no cables, no accessories", many "locked".

All ridiculous prices (like $500 for a 'like new' m3 pro MBP).

Gave this info to the police.

They could not care less.

"Well, he probably didn't steal them himself..." (i.e. even they felt pretty sure it was all stolen).

"Isn't it still a crime to knowingly sell stolen property?"

"..."

"..."

Could not care less. They had a slam dunk case in mine alone. My phone had been stolen, a police report had been filed with them, and it had shown up in California where someone who bought it on eBay gave them the seller's info, someone who lived near me.

Nope.

subscribed 2026/04/06 02:06
还有英国。

入室盗窃？盗窃？骑车人持刀抢劫？

警察会尽力阻止你举报这种情况，如果你坚持的话，你会得到犯罪号码并承诺永远不会采取任何行动。

他们甚至拒绝派巡逻队追回被盗汽车，尽管车主根据 GPS 跟踪将汽车精确定位到了特定的车库。

然而，如果你敢抗议用炸弹杀害孩子，警察会殴打你并逮捕你。

And the UK.

Burglary? Theft? Biker robbed at the knife point?

The police will try to discourage from reporting that at all, and if you insist you'll get the crime number and promise that nothing will ever be done.

They even refuse to send the patrol that could recover a stolen car despite the owner pinpointing it to the very specific garage based on the GPS tracking.

The police will, however, beat you and arrest if you dare ro protest against killing kids with bombs.

mikkupikku 2026/04/06 00:59
我真的很欣赏这种情绪，从轻率的意义上来说，我同意，但如果我们是真实的，那么至少在短期/近期内，如果社会沦为私刑暴徒，对无辜者造成伤害的可能性会更大；如果你想真实地了解这里的一些失败模式，请研究文化大革命。 另一方面，从长远来看，一段混乱时期可能是有益的。这就是加速主义者的立场。 值得考虑，但要小心这种思维方式。 为了达到不择手段的目的，许多人为了从未实现的目的而被杀害。
I really do appreciate the sentiment, and in a flippant sense I agree, but if we're being real, the potential for harm to innocents, at least in the short/near term, is far greater if society devolves into vigilante mobs; study the Cultural Revolution if you want a realistic picture of some of the failure modes here. On the other hand, a period of chaos might be beneficial in the long term; that's the accelerationist position. Worth considering, but be careful with that mode of thinking. The ends justifying the means has gotten a great many people killed for ends that never manifested.

redwall_hp 2026/04/06 01:54
促成辩诉交易制度的另一件事是保释的存在，长期以来，保释一直被批评为一种将不平等纳入法律体系的付费获胜计划。世界其他地区也认为这很奇怪。

如果他们不太可能伤害其他人，你就……不要在开庭前拘留他们。如果他们可以被保释，那么他们就没有理由不受到欺诈。

Another thing that enables the plea bargain system is the existence of bail, which has long been criticized for being a pay-to-win scheme baking inequality into the legal system. It's also seen by other parts of the world as bizarre.

You just...don't hold people before their court date if they're unlikely to harm other people. If they can be released on bail, there's no reason they shouldn't be without the grift.

在 HN 中查看
wat10000 2026/04/05 23:29
放弃权利是很奇怪的。例如，众所周知，你不能放弃不成为奴隶的权利。为什么你应该能够放弃任何权利？第六修正案没有说“除非被告不想要”。
Waiving rights is weird. It’s well understood that you can’t waive your right not to be a slave, for example. Why should you be able to waive any right? The 6th amendment doesn’t say “unless the accused doesn’t want it.”
在 HN 中查看
jjk166 2026/04/06 01:37
你可以放弃不成为奴隶的权利，但没有人可以获得合法拥有奴隶的权利。第十三修正案并没有保障人们自由生活的权利，它禁止奴隶制的做法。如果你发现有人生活在奴隶制中，你不会因为他们是奴隶而逮捕他们，你会逮捕奴役他们的人。如果有人要求你为他们做违法的事情，你有义务拒绝；即使他们是唯一直接受到影响的人并且他们坚持要求这样做，您也无权批准该请求。

您可以选择不行使任何权利，这才是权利。宗教自由并不意味着有义务信奉宗教，言论自由并不意味着强迫言论，携带武器的权利并不意味着有义务携带武器，等等。同样，您可以选择不行使获得迅速审判的权利。如果法律规定所有审判必须迅速而迅速，事情就会有所不同。

You can waive your right not to be a slave, but no one can gain the right to legally own slaves. The 13th amendment doesn't guarantee the rights of people to live free, it bans the practice of slavery. If you find someone living in slavery you don't arrest them for being a slave, you arrest the person who enslaved them. If someone asks you to do something illegal for them, you are obliged to say no; even if they are the only person directly impacted and they insist they want it, it is not in your authority to grant the request.

You can choose not to exercise any right, that's what makes it a right. Freedom of religion does not imply an obligation to practice religion, freedom of speech does not compel speech, right to bear arms does not imply an obligation to bear arms, etc. Your right to a swift and speedy trial is likewise something you can choose not to exercise. Things would be different if the law was all trials must be swift and speedy.

在 HN 中查看
wat10000 2026/04/06 01:54
按照这个逻辑，你可以放弃快速审判的权利，但国家以非快速方式审判你是违法的。
By that logic, you can waive the right to a speedy trial, but it would be illegal for the state to try you in a non-speedy fashion.

Because it's presumably beneficial. It gives your lawyers extra time to prepare for the case or to potentially settle on more favorable terms.

walletdrainer 2026/04/06 00:02

在 HN 中查看
mikkupikku 2026/04/06 00:56
总体上能够实现最公平结果的速度有待解释。 更多的时间来准备你的案子可能是好的，但是这个过程拖得越长，你在这个过程中损失的有限生命的大部分时间就越大。 对于适用于所有真实案件的普遍案件，无法客观地权衡这两个因素以及所有其他各种因素，因此无法给予被告对此事一定的发言权。
The speed which enables the most fair outcome overall is subject to interpretation. More time to prepare your case is probably good, but the longer the process drags out, the greater portion of your finite life you've lost to the process. Weighing these two factors, with all the other various factors, can't be done objectively for a generalized case that can apply to all real cases, hence giving the accused some amount of say over the matter.

wat10000 2026/04/06 03:00
这让我想起了未来世界：

“你知道作为奴隶最糟糕的事情吗？他们让你工作，但他们不付钱给你，也不让你走。”

“这就是身为奴隶的唯一意义！”

所以，是的，你可以合法地成为一名奴隶，只要你忽略了使其成为奴隶的那一部分。

This reminds me of Futurama:

"You know the worst thing about being a slave? They make you work, but they don't pay you or let you go."

"That's the only thing about being a slave!"

So yeah, you can legally be a slave as long as you leave out the one part that makes it slavery.

在 HN 中查看
_DeadFred_ 2026/04/06 02:39
在美国历史的大部分时间里，认罪交易都是非法的。

您可以通过取消“试用”税来帮助解决这个问题。目前，如果您接受抗辩，您会收到轻得多的刑罚。如果基于罪行的惩罚确实公平，法律应要求对两种情况的判决相同。

Plea deals were illegal for the majority of America's history.

You could help fix this problem by removing the 'trial' tax. Currently you receive a much lower sentence if you take the plea. If a punishment is truly fair based on the crime the sentence should be required by law to be the same in both cases.

在 HN 中查看
FireBeyond 2026/04/06 05:41
并且在世界其他大部分地区仍然是非法/未使用的。

在大多数有可能的司法管辖区，它的使用是 大规模地 更少（人均不到 1%），而且监管也更加严格，并且需要接受审查。在美国，法官表面上对认罪协议拥有否决权，但实际上这种权力几乎从未行使过，除非误判（无论哪个方向）严重到无法轻易忽视的程度。

And are still illegal/unused in the vast majority of the rest of the world.

And in most of those jurisdictions where it is a possibility, its use is massively less (less than 1% per capita) and also far more regulated, and subject to review. In the US a judge ostensibly has the power of veto over a plea deal, though in practice this is nearly never exercised unless the miscarriage of justice (in either direction) is so egregious that it can't easily be ignored.

kennywinker 2026/04/05 22:02
> 这些测试很受欢迎，因为它们便宜、便携，并且可以在短短几分钟内筛查药物。将所有可疑药物样本发送到州立实验室是不可行的，这会昂贵得多，并且可能需要数天或数周才能返回结果。

听起来像是政府的问题，而不是被指控犯罪的人的问题。测试的局限性并不能证明使用不准确的工具是合理的。如果需要几周的时间，那就需要几周的时间。政府不会仅仅因为更方便就毁掉无辜的生命。至少，他们不应该……显然他们应该这样做

> The tests are popular because they’re cheap, portable and can screen for drugs in mere minutes. It’s just not feasible to send all suspected drug samples to state laboratories, which would be far more expensive and could take days or weeks to return results.

Sounds like the govs problem, not the people accused of a crime. Limitations in testing do not justify using inaccurate tools. If it takes weeks, it takes weeks. Gov doesn’t get to ruin innocent lives just because it’s more convenient. At least, they shouldn’t… apparently they do

在 HN 中查看
t-3 2026/04/05 22:55
部分问题在于，公设辩护压力极大，报酬也不是特别高，而且不如检方享有盛誉。

很少有人会全力以赴捍卫已经“未通过”药检的人。最好快速达成认罪协议，并重点关注稍后会给私营部门客户留下深刻印象的案件。

如果你能通过一些需要罚款并忍受一些烦恼的交易而出狱，那么真的值得冒着失败的风险去证明自己的清白吗？

Part of the problem is that public defence is extremely stressful and not particularly well paid, as well as being less prestigious than prosecution.

Few are going to give their all to defend someone who has already "failed" a drug test. Better to sort out a quick plea deal and focus on cases that will impress private sector clients later.

If you can get out of jail time with some deal that requires a fine and putting up with some annoyances, is it really worth fighting things out to prove your innocence and risk failing?

kennywinker 2026/04/06 00:08
是的，在个人层面上这是有道理的——但无论是谁看到了这个测试的错误率（或者撒谎来出售它）并批准警察使用它，他们自己都应该被判入狱。

你可能会认为我们有一个机制来实现这一点。

Right, on an individual level that makes sense - but whoever saw the error rate for this test (or lied about it to sell it) and approved it for use by police deserves jail time themselves.

You’d think we’d have a mechanism to make that happen.

kennywinker 2026/04/06 04:15

在 HN 中查看
alwa 2026/04/06 01:20
而且，为了慈善起见——你的许多客户都抗议自己的清白 做过 他们实际上有一些真正积极的东西。 TFA 援引制造商的说法，称在实验室条件下误报率为 4%，而抗议者则声称在现场条件下误报率为 15-18% [*]。总体而言，令人反感且难以忍受，是的；但就我面前的任何具体案例的基本赔率而言？

假设我的 18% 的被告身上确实没有毒品或毒品残留，这仍然意味着五分之四的人身上有毒品或毒品残留——而且额外几个月的监禁和战斗对他们来说不会有任何结果。相比之下，6 个月的转移时间意味着什么？

即使可能性很大，有多少被告的说法足够精确以暗示测试的准确性 具体来说？ “不，伙计，我发誓我不再做那种事了”可能是完全正确的 仍然 对旧违禁品挎包或其他物品的拭子产生边际但确认的实验室结果。

在我看来，这就是辩诉谈判部分的目的：不是希望昂贵而缓慢的科学能给你确定性（或背景），而是“嘿检察官，即使测试是正确的，我们谈论的是残留物而不是砖块……转移注意力，就到此为止吧？”

我觉得，从外部（并不断观看《法律与秩序》电视节目），人们严重高估了刑事定罪的确定性程度。我想起了经典的 Kalven 和 Zeisel 研究 (1967) [1]，其中发现，参加完全相同审判的法官和陪审团在高达 25% 的案件中对有罪或无罪意见不一。

错误的信念是令人愤慨的，我理解我们的个人本能是说“去追求真相！”但人是复杂的，背景是模糊的，任何正义方法都存在一定的错误率，这是不可减少的。这加起来的人数比我们想象的要多得多。

不要将该人与该人的官僚记录混淆的众多原因之一。

[*] 或者，正如 CNN 人士所写，NYCDOI 的一项研究显示“错误率为 91%”……猜测是因为他们在基本率极低的情况下过度应用了测试？起初，我以为这是新闻工作者无知的典型案例，但通读研究 [0]，他们是绝对正确的，值得暂时放在一边：91% 真的是 这 实际的 实验室在实际现场测试中发现的误报率。监狱邮件拦截计划中的表面拭子，仅检查芬太尼测试，但仍然如此。这本书从各个方面来说都是一种疯狂的阅读：现场测试和纸张中的化学物质之间存在已知的反应，但他们无论如何都用它来邮寄；他们看到的误报越多，他们就越担心所有邮件中都含有芬太尼。 DOC 所有初始现场测试中 89% 的结果呈阳性， 91% 这些积极的结果被证明是错误的。政府将此解释为，所有囚犯的家人显然都在“将信封浸泡在液体芬太尼中”，然后将其邮寄给囚犯“咀嚼”。这种误解导致他们一路尝试在系统中禁止实体邮件。

[0] https://www.nyc.gov/assets/doi/reports/pdf/2024/FieldTesting...

[1] https://www.commentary.org/articles/abraham-goldstein/the-am... 并于近期复制 https://scholarship.law.cornell.edu/facpub/343/

And, to put a charitable gloss on it—many of your clients who protest their innocence did actually have something true-positive on them. TFA cites manufacturers claiming a 4% false positive rate in lab conditions, and protesters claiming a 15-18% FPR in field conditions [*]. Offensive and intolerable in the aggregate, yes; but as far as base odds for any specific case in front of me?

Assuming 18% of my defendants truly didn’t have drugs or drug residue on them, that still means 4 out of 5 of them did—and that the extra months of sitting in jail and fighting would lead nowhere for them. What’s 6 months of diversion by comparison?

Even beyond the odds, how many of defendants’ claims are precise enough to implicate test accuracy specifically? “No, man, I swear I don’t do that stuff anymore” might be completely true and still result in a marginal-but-confirmatory lab result for the swab of the ol’ contraband satchel or whatever.

Seems to me that’s the sort of stuff that the plea-negotiation part is for: instead of hoping expensive and slow science will give you certainty (or context), it’s “hey prosecutor, even if the test is right, we’re talking residue not bricks… diversion and call it a day?”

I feel like, from the outside (and fed a steady diet of Law and Order television show), people grossly overestimate the degree of certainty embedded in a criminal conviction. I’m reminded of the classic Kalven and Zeisel study (1967) [1] finding, among other things, that judges and juries who sat through the exact same trial disagreed on guilt or innocence in up to 25% of cases.

False convictions are outrageous, and I understand our individual instincts here to say “go to the mat for the truth!” But people are complicated, context is hazy, and there’s some irreducible residual rate at which any approach to justice gets it wrong. Which adds up to a lot more individuals than we’d like to assume.

One of many reasons not to confuse the person with the bureaucratic record of the person.

[*] Or, as the CNN person writes, a “91% error rate” per an NYCDOI study… guessing because there they were over-applying the tests in situations with extremely low base rates? At first I assumed this was a typical case of journalistic innumeracy, but reading through the study [0], they’re absolutely right and worth a quick aside: 91% really was the actual false positive rate the lab found in actual field tests. Surface swabs in a jail mail interdiction program, only examining the fentanyl tests, but still. It’s a wild read in all sorts of ways: there was a known reaction between the field test and chemicals in paper, but they used it for the mail anyway; the more false positives they saw, the more they panicked that all the mail had fentanyl in it. 89% of all the initial DOC field tests came back positive, 91% of those positives turned out to be false ones. The administration interpreted this to mean that all the inmates’ families were clearly “soaking envelopes in liquid fentanyl,” then mailing them to inmates to “chew.” The misconception led them all the way to trying to ban physical mail in the system.

[0] https://www.nyc.gov/assets/doi/reports/pdf/2024/FieldTesting...

[1] https://www.commentary.org/articles/abraham-goldstein/the-am... and replicated recently https://scholarship.law.cornell.edu/facpub/343/

kennywinker 2026/04/06 04:20
我可能会接受 0.1% 的误报率，但 4% 的误报率是不合理的。是的，试验很混乱，但这不是试验——这是一次糟糕的测试。

说实话，你的“人很复杂”的立场感觉就像是一个相信自己在这种情况下永远不会站在错误一边的人的立场。我希望你永远不会这样做，但如果你包括那些可能接受此类测试的群体（例如监狱囚犯），我认为这个立场是站不住脚的。

I might accept a 0.1% false positive rate - but 4% is unconscionable. Yes, trials are messy, but this isn’t a trial - it’s a bad test.

Tbh, your “people are complicated” stance feels like the position of someone who believes they will never be on the wrong side of situations like these. I hope you never are, but I don’t think it’s a position that is defensible if you’re including people who are in groups likely to be subjected to tests like this (e.g. prison inmates)

swores 2026/04/05 23:11

政府的任何部门都很少将其作为预期目的。

但不幸的是，对于参与其中的人来说，愿意接受这一点是非常常见的，因为他们在追求任何目标时都会产生副作用——无论是为警察部门获得资金，还是从私人监狱的所有者那里筹集政治捐款，或者让穷人远离他们美丽的中上层阶级社区，或者破坏环境的化学公司，或者……等等。

Sorry but that's bullshit.

It's extremely rare for any part of government to have that as an intended purpose.

But it's extremely common, unfortunately, for people involved to be willing to accept that as a side effect in pursuing whatever their goals are - whether that's gaining funding for their police department, or raising political donations from the owners of a private prison, or keeping poor people away from their beautiful upper middle class neighbourhood, or environment-ruining chemical company, or... whatever.

在 HN 中查看
20after4 2026/04/05 23:33
系统的目的是它做什么，而不是它声称做什么。
A system’s purpose is what it does, not what it claims to do.
在 HN 中查看
therealdrag0 2026/04/06 01:23 deleted
[已标记]

在 HN 中查看
array_key_first 2026/04/06 03:51
这确实有道理，因为如果你正在开发一个坏的或邪恶的系统，那么你显然会想尽可能地混淆它。显然，你要做的第一件事就是宣称该系统的目的是好的。

这是一个常见的谬误，或者我想这可能是我经常看到的拙劣的推理。因为某人或某物要么不表明自己的意图，要么说自己的意图是好的，那么他所使用的东西也一定是好的。或者，我们必须假设它是好的，直到他们宣布他们将把它用于不良目的。

就像羊群一样。有很多人认为弗洛克认为用于打击犯罪的简单防御意味着它很好。或者狗狗。 DOGE 背后的人说这是为了防止欺诈，这一简单的辩护意味着它是好的。

但人们所说的和实际发生的事情是两件不同的事情，而实际发生的部分要重要 1000 倍。任何人都可以说任何话，显然坏演员会撒谎。这只是给定的。因此，您不能使用某物的既定目的来为该物辩护。你就是不能，这毫无意义。

It does make sense, because if you were developing a bad or evil system then you would obviously want to obfuscate that as much as possible. The first thing you'd do, clearly, is proclaim that the purpose of the system is something good.

This is a common fallacy or I guess maybe shoddy reasoning I see often. Because someone or something either does not announce their intentions or says their intentions are good, then the thing they are using must also be good. Or, we must assume it is good until they announce they're going to use it for not-good purposes.

Like with Flock. There's a lot of people who think the simple defense that Flock thinks it is used to fight crime means it's good. Or DOGE. The simple defense that the people behind DOGE say it's to prevent fraud means it's good.

But what people say and what actually happens are two different things, and the what actually happens part is 1000x more important. Anyone can say anything, and obviously bad actors will lie. That's just a given. So you can't use the stated purpose of something as a defense for that something. You just can't, it makes no sense.

在 HN 中查看
therealdrag0 2026/04/06 06:09
我从未说过既定目的是辩护。目的只是目的而不是结果。仅仅因为你无法知道每个参与者的真实意图，并不意味着你有理由假设其目的就是它所做的。那是懒惰的、破碎的认识论。

我同意系统所做的事情才是重要的，那么为什么要用意图假设和圆滑的道德说教思想来终止陈词滥调来淡化这一事实呢？

I never said the stated purpose is a defense. The purpose is the purpose not the outcome. Just because you can’t know the true intent of every actor involved does not mean you’re justified in assuming the purpose is what it does. That is lazy broken epistemology.

I agree what a system does is what is important, so why dilute that fact with assumptions of intent and glib moralizing thought terminating cliches?

kennywinker 2026/04/06 04:10
在不改变任何内容的情况下产生“意外”结果需要多长时间才能说这是系统的预期部分？因为我认为这就是问题所在。这并不是说美国的目标是将贫困定为犯罪——宪法对此没有任何规定——但由于这种情况已经持续了很长时间，该制度似乎不愿意采取必要措施来防止这种情况发生。这是系统预期行为的一部分。
How long is it ok to produce “unintended” results without changing anything, before you can say that’s now an expected part of the system? Because i think that’s the issue. It’s not that the US has a goal to criminalize poverty - the constitution doesn’t say anything about that - but since it’s been that way for so long it seems the system is unwilling to do what needs to be done to prevent that. It’s part of the expected behavior of the system.

在 HN 中查看
ahhhhnoooo 2026/04/06 11:27
公平的。

意图-什么人 通缉 或者 预期的 系统要做的事。

目的 - 系统在实践中做什么。原因或主要功能。

一些经典的例子——便利贴原本是用作航空航天粘合剂，但后来发现它们的用途是低粘纸。

如果您想要一个经典的系统示例，标准化测试是目的和意图之间差异的一个很好的例子。它的目的是成为衡量学校并确保每个孩子获得平等教育的机制。但现在它的目的可以被描述为公制学校游戏。它缩小了课程范围，鼓励应试教学。这些结果并不是最初的意图。或者甚至是令人向往的。

所以当我说意图和目的不同时，我并不是轻率（也许有点轻率）。

其他经典例子——美国参议院、社交媒体算法、动物赏金（按人头奖励杀死老鼠、青蛙或蛇的人会导致人们繁殖这些动物）、禁毒战争、分区法等。

这与“通往地狱的道路是由善意铺成的”这一理念密切相关。

Fair.

Intent - what someone wanted or expected the system to do.

Purpose - what the system does in practice. The reason, or primary function for it.

Some classic examples -- post it notes were intended as a aerospace adhesive, but found their purpose as low tack papers.

If you want a classic systems example, standardized testing is a good example of difference between purpose and intent. It was intended to be a mechanism for measuring schools and ensuring every kid got an equal education. But now its purpose could be described as the metric schools game. It narrows curricula, encourages teaching to the test. Those outcomes are not the original intent. Or even desirable.

So I wasn't being flippant (maybe a little flippant) when I was saying intent and purpose are different.

Other classic examples -- the US senate, social media algorithms, animal bounties (paying people per head bounties on killed rats, frogs, or snakes results in people breeding those animals), war on drugs, zoning laws, etc.

It's very closely related to the idea that "the road to hell is paved with good intentions".

在 HN 中查看
anonymousiam 2026/04/05 23:49
这并不像你想象的那么罕见。

OSF/OSI（开放社会基金会，而不是开放软件基金会）等组织已成功地将其首选候选人安排在美国许多主要司法管辖区的权力职位上。 如果您进行研究，您会看到许多 OSF DA 根据其政治意识形态起诉或不起诉的案例。 许多起诉都是出于政治动机，但现在我们有基金会资助积极推动同一议程的候选人。 结果是对政府的信任度下降，活动人士将利用这种信任最终使事情变得更糟，因为“资本主义不起作用”。

It's not as rare as you might think.

Organizations such as OSF/OSI (Open Society Foundations, not Open Software Foundation) have successfully placed their preferred candidates in positions of power in many major US jurisdictions. If you research, you'll see many cases of OSF DAs prosecuting or not prosecuting based on their political ideology. Many prosecutions are politically motivated, but now we have foundations funding activist candidates who are all pushing the same agenda. The result is diminished trust in government, which the activists will exploit to eventually make things even worse, because "capitalism is not working."

在 HN 中查看
kennywinker 2026/04/06 00:18
你说得好像他们在腐败。 IE。不要起诉你的朋友，而要起诉你的敌人。但这更像是利用你管辖范围内的权力来反对不公正的法律。

IE。在我居住的地方，市议会很久以前就指示警方停止逮捕持有大麻的人，理由是这是一项不公正的法律，将其定为刑事犯罪会占用资源，弊大于利，而且该市大多数人口都支持合法化。市政府无权改变这些法律，但他们可以通过指导执法人员在当地解决这些问题。十年后，它合法化了——我认为证明这是正确的决定。

这并没有“减少对政府的信任”。事实上，在我看来，大多数人不同意但仍保留在案文中的法律对政府信誉的损害要大得多

You make it sound like they are doing corruption. I.e. don’t prosecute your friends, do prosecute your enemies. But this is more like using the power at your jurisdiction level to oppose unjust laws.

I.e. where i live the city council long ago directed police to stop arresting people for marijuana possession - on the grounds that this is an unjust law and criminalizing it is tying up resources and doing more harm than good, and because the majority of the city’s population supports legalization. City gov doesn’t have the power to change those laws, but they can fix it locally by directing enforcement away from them. A decade later, it was legalized - imo proving that it was the right decision.

This did not “diminish trust” in the gov. In fact, laws that the majority disagree with but stay on the books do far far more damage to the credibility of gov, in my opinion

kennywinker 2026/04/06 10:00
事实上，DA自由裁量权是政府运作的正常组成部分。书本上每天都有一千条法律被忽视 [1]。每次选举时，候选人都会在承诺“打击”这种或那种犯罪的平台上竞选（阅读：有选择地加强执法）。

政府执行大多数人不希望的法律是对民主的颠覆，它使人们疏远了政府可以做出回应而不是压迫的想法。我不相信一个声称代表人民意愿，但却指控人们犯下大多数人不认为是犯罪的罪行的政府。

所以也许 你 当你看到你想要执行的法律被搁置时，你就会更少信任政府，但你在这里属于少数。我怎么知道？因为这些 DA 正在变得 当选 （未安装）来执行此操作。

Actually, DA discretion is a normal part of the functioning of government. There are a thousand laws on the books that get ignored every day [1]. And every election, candidates run on platforms promising to “crack down” on this or that crime (read: selectively increase enforcement).

Gov enforcing laws that the majority of people do not want is a subversion of democracy that alienates people from the idea that gov can be responsive instead of oppressive. I don’t trust a gov that claims to represent the will of the people, but charges people for crimes most don’t see as criminal.

So maybe you trust a gov less when you see laws you want enforced being set aside, but you’re in the minority here. How do i know? Because these DAs are getting elected (not installed) to do this.

[1] https://www.msn.com/en-us/society-culture-and-history/social...

Aurornis 2026/04/05 23:07
所有简单的药物测试均旨在用作筛查工具，并将阳性结果发送至实验室进行验证。

> 甚至比色测试制造商也表示，他们的产品只能筛查非法药物的可能性，不应被视为验证工具。

> “注意：所有测试结果必须由批准的分析实验室确认！”读取一组比色测试的警告。

他们应该知道这一点并遵循适当的程序。

在您的就业药物筛查中也请记住这一点。这通常会在第一次通过时使用更高级的测试，但如果有人呈阳性，那么他们应该自动将其发送到更高级和准确的筛选步骤。好的测试公司会自动执行此操作，但我听说一些廉价的测试公司没有这样做，并且错误地指责员工不知道要求准确的筛选步骤。

All of the simple drug tests are intended for use as screening tools, with positive results sent to labs for verification.

> Even colorimetric test makers say their products only screen for the possibility of illegal drugs – and should not be considered tools for verification.

> “NOTE: ALL TEST RESULTS MUST BE CONFIRMED BY AN APPROVED ANALYTICAL LABORATORY!” reads one warning for a pack of colorimetric tests.

They should have known this and followed proper procedure.

Also keep this in mind for your employment drug screening. This will typically use more advanced tests for the first pass, but if someone comes up positive then they should automatically send it to the more advanced and accurate screening step. The good testing companies do this automatically but I’ve heard stories where some cheap testing companies did not do this and falsely accused employees didn’t know to request the accurate screening step.

在 HN 中查看
beej71 2026/04/06 00:22
我怀疑如果你不允许根据这些测试进行逮捕并要求实验室进行后续调查，那么这些测试将完全停止使用。

警察知道误报率，他们将不再浪费时间，而是依靠他们的训练和直觉。

这里也存在自动化偏差的影响。 “它回来时是蓝色的，所以我知道蓝色包告诉我我应该逮捕，所以我可以逮捕。如果是错的，那不是我的错。”

更进一步说，如果法律规定，如果出现误报，嫌疑人每入狱一天，逮捕警察就必须在监狱里待一天，那么没有警察敢使用这种测试。这表明了他们实际上对此的信任程度。

I suspect if you disallow arrests based on these tests and require a lab followup, the tests will cease to be used entirely.

The police know the false positive rate and they'll stop wasting their time and rely on their training and instincts, instead.

There's an implication of automation bias here, too. "It came back blue, so I can just make an arrest knowing that the blue bag told me I should. Not my fault if it's wrong."

Pushing farther, if the law said that if there was a false positive, the arresting officer would have to spend one day in jail per day the suspect was jailed, no cop would ever dare use this test. That demonstrates the amount of trust they actually have in it.

ahhhhnoooo 2026/04/06 00:38
系统的目的就是它的作用。在这里，毒品法导致了数以万计的错误逮捕，其中一些人即使是无辜的也会接受认罪协议。通常是因为他们收入低，需要摆脱困境，以免失去工作。

相反，他们会得到记录（免费）并冒着入狱的风险。他们无力证明自己的清白。

The purpose of the system is what it does. Here, drug laws result in tens of thousands of wrongful arrests, some portion of which will take the plea deal even though they are innocent. Typically because they are low income and need to get out of holding so they don't lose their job.

Instead they get a record (for nothing) and risk prison. They cannot afford to prove their innocence.

在 HN 中查看
_DeadFred_ 2026/04/06 02:47
不要忘记“踪迹税”。如果您行使审判权，您的刑期为 10-20-50 年，而如果您“自愿”放弃权利，那么您的刑期可能为 1-5 年。即使你是无辜的，你现在在监狱里已经失去了工作、汽车、房子，你会冒着一个已经对你做了这些事的系统会给你公平审判的风险吗？或者你会放弃过去的经验，即系统会操你，而只采取较短的一点？
Don't forget the 'trail tax'. Sentences are 10-20-50 years if you exercise your right to trial, while if you 'voluntarily' waive your right the plea put before you could be 1-5 years. Even if you are innocent, you are now at the point while in jail you already lost your job, your car, your house, are you going to risk that a system that did that to you already is going to give you a fair trial? Or are you going to go off of past experience that system is going to fuck you and just take the shorter bit?

可以说是什么杀死了亚伦·斯沃茨：

>2012 年 9 月 12 日，联邦检察官提交了一份替代起诉书，增加了 9 项重罪罪名，将斯沃茨的最高刑事风险提高至 50 年监禁和 100 万美元罚款。[16][104][105]在与斯沃茨的律师进行认罪谈判期间，如果斯沃茨承认犯有 13 项联邦罪行，检察官建议在低安全级别的监狱中判处六个月的徒刑。

arguably what killed Aaron Swartz:

>On September 12, 2012, federal prosecutors filed a superseding indictment adding nine more felony counts, increasing Swartz's maximum criminal exposure to 50 years of imprisonment and $1 million in fines.[16][104][105] During plea negotiations with Swartz's attorneys, the prosecutors offered to recommend a sentence of six months in a low-security prison if Swartz pled guilty to 13 federal crimes.

AlBugdy 2026/04/05 22:22
据我所知，某些类型的药物测试无法衡量您现在是否吸毒过多，或者您以前是否服用过药物并且现在是否清醒。如果您清醒驾驶，但昨天服用了毒品，您可能会因酒后驾车而被捕。

如果这些测试不能可靠地表明您在进行测试时处于兴奋状态，请不要使用它们。

对于除了驾驶或操作重型机械等以外的任何事情，此类测试根本没有意义。让人们想吃什么药就吃什么药。就像我们对酒精和香烟等合法药物所做的那样——监管质量、购买时需要身份证并对其征税。

这显然会减少有组织犯罪。如果你让卖淫合法化，你会进一步降低它。仍然会有人试图以比受管制药品便宜的价格出售劣质自制药品——就像我们有非法香烟一样，但这与我们现在拥有的相比根本不算什么。

让毒品不再成为禁忌。教育人们如何减少伤害，并让人们在遇到问题时更容易承认。

作为一个没有受过医学教育的受过一定教育的人，我几乎已经承担了天底下的一切，并且仍然在社会中运作良好。负责任地使用药物确实是可能的。如果你的印象是一个衣衫褴褛的瘾君子躺在桥下的旧床垫上，共用一根肮脏的针头，那么你看到的只是没有受过教育、没有社会安全网的人。不管你相信与否，受过教育的吸毒者无处不在。我们只是不经常谈论它，就像我们不会在工作或学术界随意向其他人提及我们的恋物癖一样。

但毒品和性很有趣，也许太有趣了，我们不能让公民过分享受。 :/

AFAIK some types of drug tests can't measure whether you're high on $drug now or if you're taken it before and you're sober now. If you're driving sober but you've taken $drug yesterday, you might be arrested for DWI.

If these tests can't reliably show you're high at the time the test was taken, don't use them.

For anything other than driving or operating heavy machinery and so on, there's no point in such tests at all. Let people take whatever drugs they want. Just do what we do with legal drugs like alcohol and cigarettes - regulate the quality, require an ID card for purchase and tax them.

This will obviously lower organized crime. If you make prostitution legal, you'll lower it even more. There will still be people trying to sell their shitty home made drugs cheaper than the regulated ones - like we have illegal cigarettes, but that's nothing compared to what we have now.

Make drugs less of a taboo. Educate people on harm reduction and make it easy to admit when you have a problem with something.

As a somewhat-educated person without medical education, I've taken almost everything under the sun and still function well within society. It's really possible to use drugs responsibly. If the image you have is a junkie with ragged clothes lying on some old mattress under a bridge sharing a dirty needle, you're only looking at the uneducated people with no safety net from society. Believe it or not, educated drug users are everywhere. We just don't often talk about it like we don't casually mention our fetishes to others in work or academia.

But drugs and sex are fun, maybe too fun, and we can't let the citizens enjoy themselves too much. :/

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/05 23:12
> 据我所知，某些类型的药物测试无法衡量您现在是否吸毒过多，或者您以前是否服用过药物并且现在是否清醒。如果您清醒驾驶，但昨天服用了毒品，您可能会因酒后驾车而被捕。

对于寻找半衰期较长的 THC 代谢物的 THC（大麻）测试来说就是如此。身体需要很长时间才能通过不同的步骤代谢 THC，然后消除这些代谢物。

对于文章中提到的许多药物来说，它们的消除速度很快。对于芬太尼等药物，它们的浓度也很低。如果一个人体内的芬太尼浓度很高，可以通过简单的测试检测到，那么他就已经喝醉了。

> AFAIK some types of drug tests can't measure whether you're high on $drug now or if you're taken it before and you're sober now. If you're driving sober but you've taken $drug yesterday, you might be arrested for DWI.

This is true for THC (marijuana) tests that look for metabolites of THC with long half-lives. It takes a very long time for the body to metabolize THC through the different steps and then eliminate those metabolites.

For many of the drugs named in the article their elimination is rapid. For some like fentanyl their concentrations are also low. If someone has an appreciable concentration of fentanyl detectable by a simple test they are very inebriated.

drfloyd51 2026/04/05 22:32
如果你将资金与逮捕联系起来，就会逮捕更多人。

也许将资金转移到有罪判决上会有所帮助？

至少有一个客观的第三者必须同意这一指控。

当然……法官和警察是由同一实体支付报酬的。法官和警察通过工作相互认识。

If you relate funding to arrests, more arrests will be made.

Perhaps shifting funding to relate to guilty verdicts could help?

At least then there is an objective 3rd party that has to agree with the charge.

Of course… the judges and the cops are paid by the same entity. And the judges and the cops know each other through their work.

在 HN 中查看
projektfu 2026/04/05 22:42
从根本上来说，问题是我们逮捕了太多人。 警察因怀疑而当场逮捕某人，只有在认为某人危及他人或可能伤害更多人时才应进行。 如果携带少量毒品的人没有明显受损，就不会这样做。

如果他们想取样作为证据并进行测试，可以等待几周进行真正的测试，然后他们可以发出法官令。

我们确实需要开始要求这成为美国的常态。

Fundamentally, the problem is that we have too many arrests. An officer arresting someone on the spot because of suspicion should only be done when they are assumed to be endangering people or likely to be victimizing more people. Someone carrying a small amount of drugs is not doing that if they are not visibly impaired.

If they want to take a sample into evidence and have it tested, it can wait a few weeks for real testing and then they can issue a bench warrant.

We really need to start asking for this to be the norm in the US.

在 HN 中查看
swores 2026/04/05 22:56
我同意你在“保持毒品政策大致相同但改善治安”的背景下的想法。

但操现在的毒品政策。如果某人吸食大麻、摇头丸，甚至是可卡因和海洛因等“更难”的药物，只要它不会导致他们变得暴力或犯下盗窃等其他犯罪行为，那么为什么这对他们来说会比他们因花几个小时喝啤酒而喝醉而更容易成为法律问题呢？除了保留现有的“酒后驾车”法律等奇怪的例外之外，我们不需要专门的“因毒品而暴力”或“因毒品而盗窃”的立法，无论是否涉及毒品，这些犯罪都应受到同等对待。

当前的“禁毒战争”未能阻止人们购买和使用消遣性毒品，也未能保护社会和社会成员免受自身和他人的侵害，因为证据和专家都指出，投资于提供医疗服务（康复、治疗等）比警务和监狱服务能带来更好的结果。

我非常认为，如果到那时我们没有意外地杀死我们的物种，那么将来有一天，我们会像我们大多数人现在看待历史上的奴隶制类型或用石头砸死同性恋者一样，回顾这一时期，认为这既愚蠢又令人难以置信的不道德。我希望我们在未来几年/几十年内达到这一点，而不是需要几个世纪。

（需要明确的是，我写“历史性奴隶制”并不是暗示有某些形式的现代奴隶制我认为是可以接受的；而是因为可悲的是，现代奴隶制仍然在世界各地持续存在，所以我们不能说这是一种普遍谴责的过去的事情。希望有一天它也会如此。）

I agree with your thinking in the context of "keeping drug policy broadly the same but improving policing of it".

But fuck current drug policy. If someone is high on cannabis, or MDMA, or even "harder" drugs like cocaine and heroin, as long as it's not causing them to be violent or to commit other crimes such as theft then why should that become a legal problem for them any more than if they were intoxicated from spending a few hours drinking beers? And with the odd exception like keeping existing "driving under the influence" laws, we don't need dedicated "violent because of drugs" or "theft because of drugs" legislation, those crime should be treated the same regardless of whether drugs were involved or not.

The current "war on drugs" has been a failure at preventing people from buying and using recreational drugs, and it's been a failure at protecting society and members of society from themselves and from others, because evidence and experts all points towards better results coming from investing in being able to offer medical treatment services (rehab, therapy, etc) than policing and prison services.

I very much think there will come a time in the future, if we haven't accidentally killed our species by then, that we look back on this time the same way as most of us currently look at historic types of slavery, or stoning homosexuals to death, as both idiotic and incredible immoral. I hope we reach that point in the coming years/decades, rather than needing centuries.

(To be clear, I wrote "historic slavery" not to imply there are some forms of modern slavery that I think are OK; but because sadly there is still modern slavery ongoing all around the world, so we can't say that it's a universally condemned thing of the past. Hopefully one day it will be, too.)

__MatrixMan__ 2026/04/05 22:44
如果您出于安全目的尝试使用它们，它们也非常糟糕。 就像……紫色在不再是紫色之前可以有多蓝，我不知道。 去年它是非常紫色的，但现在测试已经一年了，或者是产品不均匀，这就是为什么它不同......

基本上，如果您对任何情况注入足够的怀疑，比色测试最终会让您相信自己遇到了麻烦。 然后，如果您将样本带给能够使用真实设备的人，通常会发现除了您最初寻找的那种问题之外，没有任何问题。

老实说，我认为只要在图书馆 3D 打印机旁边放置一台气相色谱仪并培训人们使用它，我们就可以挽救很多生命。

They're also pretty awful if you try to use them for safety purposes. Like... how blue can purple be before it's not purple anymore, idk. Last year it was very purple, but now the test is a year older, or is the product non-uniform and that's why it's different...

Basically, if you inject enough suspicion into any situation, colorimetric tests will eventually lead you to believe that you're in trouble. And then later if you bring the sample to somebody with access to real equipment it often turns out there was no trouble besides the sort you were looking for in the first place.

I honestly think we could save a lot of lives by just putting a gas chromatography machine in the library next to the 3d printers and training people to use it.

在 HN 中查看
Atheros 2026/04/06 02:47
这上面写满了基本利率谬误。

如果您有一台醉酒驾驶员检测机器，当驾驶员喝醉时，95% 的时间输出“阳性”，当驾驶员未醉酒时，100% 的时间输出“阴性”，并且开始对道路上的所有驾驶员进行测试，那么阳性检测准确的概率仅为 1.96%。这听起来和正在发生的事情一模一样：对鸟粪和小老太太进行拉网测试。

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_rate_fallacy#Example_2:_D...

如果人们很难理解简单的统计数据，那么基本利率谬误就正确了。

This has Base Rate Fallacy written all over it.

If you had a drunk-driver-detection machine that output "positive" 95% of the time when the driver is drunk and output "negative" 100% of the time when the driver is not drunk, and started administering the test to all drivers on a road, the probability that a positive detection is accurate is only 1.96%. That sounds exactly like what was happening: dragnet testing of bird droppings and little old ladies.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_rate_fallacy#Example_2:_D...

If simple statistics are difficult for people to understand, the Base Rate Fallacy is right out.

在 HN 中查看
pandaman 2026/04/06 08:18
我不跟。维基百科中的示例使用与您给出的错误率相反的错误率：它在 5% 的情况下错误地标记了清醒的驾驶员。它之所以有效，是因为规定的醉酒率远低于 5% (0.1%)，因此假阳性结果压倒了真阳性结果。在您的设置中，阳性检测的准确度应为 95%，阴性错误（醉酒司机被视为清醒）约为 0.005%，您从哪里得到 1.96%？

此外，正如维基百科文章指出的，这假设进行了全面测试。我非常怀疑警察会四处走动并对他们看到的每个人进行药物测试。

I don't follow. The example in the wikipedia uses the opposite error rates to what you have given: it falsely flags a sober driver in 5% of cases. And it works because the stipulated rate of drunkenness is much lower than 5% (0.1%) so the false positives overwhelm the true positives. In your setup the positive detection should be accurate at 95%, the negative error (a drunk driver passed as sober) - would be around 0.005%, where did you get 1.96%?

Furthermore, as Wikipedia article noted, this assumes total testing. I highly doubt cops are walking around and drug test everyone they see.

but even the pricey tests can wrongfully have positives. if you eat sweets in which poppy seeds are ingredients you test positive for opioids (although near to the detection limit)
在 HN 中查看
AdrianB1 2026/04/06 00:51
这看起来像是一个典型的“除非证明有罪，否则有罪”的案例。逮捕决不应该仅仅基于“可能是”，而应该基于一些确凿的证据。当实验室确认问题时，而不是当有迹象表明可能存在问题时，进行逮捕要安全得多。
It looks like a classic case of "guilty until proven otherwise". Arrests should never be made based just on "it may be", but on some solid evidence. It is much safer to make the arrest when the lab confirms the problem, not when there is a hint there may be one.
